entries.title: Commentaries on the Twelve Davidic Psalms on psalms 37:34
entries.excerpt:
(Verse 34.) Therefore, wait either for a helper or for the Lord as a reward: And keep His ways; that is, to wait and to keep the commandments of the one whom you think you need to wait for. Even if you are worn out, even if you are troubled, even if you lose strength due to sickness, still wait for the Lord, and your hope will not be in vain.

The Lord will come and exalt you, that you may possess the land as an inheritance. It is clear that the land is superior, not this earthly valley; but that which is the promise of eternity, in which whoever is established is exalted by the Lord.

But you will see when sinners perish; then indeed there will be a reward for the righteous, when judgment is made about the wicked's merits. However, sinners do not perish in the same way. They perish for you beforehand, if you do not marvel at their power and wealth: if it does not move you when you learn that they abound in honors, children, and friends. For these things are of the world, and the world is subject to worldly malice: and the prince of the world favors those who serve him, according to that: 'All these things I will give you, if you fall down and worship me' (Matth. IV, 9). What do you seek for those things to be given to you, when the very impious one who gives cannot himself abide in them for long? He himself also passes away, so how can the things he has conferred not also pass away? Just maintain a steady mind, hold on to justice, let no one sway you, and you will see that there is nothing impious.

entries.title: COMMENTARY ON THE PSALMS 37 on psalms 37:34
entries.excerpt: The law of God is in the heart [of the righteous person], and his step is not supplanted. The devil considers him and desires to humiliate him, but God does not abandon him or curse him when he is judged. Await the Lord and guard his ways so you may inherit the land; in time you will see sinners perish.

entries.title: Exposition on Psalm 37 on psalms 37:34
entries.excerpt: "Wait on the Lord" (ver. 34). And while I am waiting upon Him, what am I to do?-"and keep His ways." And if I keep them, what am I to receive? "And He shall exalt thee to inherit the land." "What land"? Once more let not any estate suggest itself to your mind:-the land of which it is said, "Come, ye blessed of My Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world." What of those who have troubled us, in the midst of whom we have groaned, whose scandals we have patiently endured, for whom, while they were raging against us, we have prayed in vain? What will become of them? What follows? "When the wicked are cut off, thou shall see it."...

entries.title: COMMENTARY ON PSALMS 37:34A-C on psalms 37:34
entries.excerpt: Since, then, God does not allow the righteous, even if vulnerable to sinners, to be subject completely to their verdict, do not be despondent if ever you fall foul of disaster; instead, expect help from God by observing his decrees and commands, being attentive to his good pleasure and not withdrawing from virtue on account of the hardships besetting you. "And he will exalt you to inherit the land": if you do this, he will shelter you, even if vulnerable to the sinner, and make you exalted by ensuring you secure occupancy of the land. "In the destruction of sinners you will see": not only will you be freed from their scheming, but also you will see them destroyed.

entries.title: Exposition on the Psalms of David on psalms 37:34
entries.excerpt: "Wait." The Psalmist has said much in the foregoing to commend the just and to bring down the wicked; and he has directed all of this to the end that we may follow justice and turn away from evil. Here, however, he introduces an exhortation to justice. Justice is observed in two areas: namely, in those things that concern God, and in those that concern one's neighbor. Regarding this he does three things. First, he proposes the exhortation; second, he shows the reward, at "And he will exalt you"; third, he proves something he proposed, at "I saw the wicked." He directs the exhortation toward God. First, through expectation; second, through obedience. He says therefore, "Wait for the Lord," where he says two things. First, that if you suffer some trouble, do not immediately despair or be disturbed against God, but wait until he comes to your aid. Ps. 39: "Waiting I waited for the Lord, and he attended to me." And thus he exhorts to patience. Second, he exhorts to long-suffering; as if to say, if you have not yet been rewarded for your good deeds, do not be broken, but wait for the reward. Hab. 2: "If it delays, wait for it," etc. Jas. 5: "The farmer waits for the precious fruit of the earth, bearing it patiently." "And keep his ways." Expectation is in the midst of evils; keeping is in the midst of goods. Therefore keep his ways, namely of charity and of the commandments, and guard them with diligence. Prov. 4: "With all watchfulness, guard," etc. Job 23: "My foot has followed in his steps; I have kept his way." Ps. 118: "I will search out your law," etc. "And he will exalt you." Here the reward or fruit is set forth. This is the fruit of the good, that they be exalted by him. Sir. 15: "He will exalt him among his neighbors." Wis. 19: "In all things you have honored your people." And for what purpose? "That you may inherit the land." It is remarkable what he says: since the earth is the lowest thing, how will he exalt, so that you may take it? And it must be said that the land of which he speaks is the land of the living; and this is on high, as it were the stability of eternal and spiritual goods. And one does not reach it as long as the mind lies in the depths; rather, one must be exalted. Is. 58: "I will raise you up above the heights of the earth." Or it can also be understood of this earth. Therefore those inherit the earth who have dominion over the earth; and such are those who have their heart raised above the earth, because they do not have their heart pressed down to the earth, as do the slaves of the earth and those possessed by the earth. And therefore, in order to inherit it, they must be exalted. Prov. 4: "You will be glorified by her when you embrace her," etc. "When sinners perish, you will see." They will rejoice over the destruction of the wicked. Ps. 17: "The just man will rejoice when he sees the vindication," etc. Job 22: "The just will see and rejoice." But not on account of the destruction of the wicked, because God does not rejoice in it. Wis. 1: "He does not rejoice in the destruction of men"; but over divine justice. Is. 66: "They shall go out and see the corpses of the men who transgressed against me." Likewise over the divine mercy shown to them, by which they were freed from evils, or from the punishments of the damned. And to see this, one must also be exalted, because if you are caught below, you will not see their destruction. Ps. 72: "This is a labor before me," namely the deeds of sinners, "until," etc.
